topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email glowed on the screen, a stark white rectangle against the dull grey of my desk. My fingers hovered over the mouse, trembling slightly before finally clicking to open the attachment. The words swam before my eyes: "We regret to inform you that your application for the [Scholarship Name] has not been selected for this cycle." A heavy, sinking feeling settled in my stomach, a cold weight that made my chest tighten. I reread the paragraph about the decision, trying to find some flaw in my own meticulous essay, but every sentence felt hollow. I quickly minimized the window, shoving the laptop lid shut with a decisive snap. I stood up, pacing the small confines of my room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I needed air, needed to move, anything to escape the suffocating realization that all that late-night research and passionate writing had amounted to nothing.

[story 2]

I stared at the printed notification, the official letter crisp and unforgiving on the wooden table. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did nothing to settle the churning in my gut. I picked up a pen, but the ink seemed to blur as I tried to form a coherent thought. It felt like a physical blow, the kind that steals the breath right out of your lungs. I traced the lines of the rejection letter with a fingertip, the texture of the paper rough beneath my skin. When my roommate walked in, humming some tune, I instinctively tried to hide the paper under a stack of textbooks, my shoulders hunching forward as if trying to shrink into the furniture. "Rough day?" she asked, noticing my rigid posture. I managed a tight, strained smile, the muscles in my face feeling too tight, and mumbled something about a difficult assignment, acutely aware of the sudden, unwelcome heat rising in my cheeks.

[story 3]

The notification popped up with a jarring lack of fanfare. My hands, which had been steady moments before, began to shake noticeably as I read the final line. It was a definitive closure, a slamming door on a future I had so fiercely envisioned. A strange dryness coated my throat, making it difficult to swallow. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to look anywhere but at the screen—to focus on the pattern of dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunlight filtering through the window. I walked to the window, leaning my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself against the sudden instability inside. The world outside seemed vibrant and indifferent, a sharp contrast to the dull, heavy disappointment pooling in my core. I pulled back from the glass slowly, my gaze fixed on the floor, a deep sense of deflation settling over me like a heavy blanket.
